---
title: "Eigenvalue methods for repeated game analysis"
description: "Use eigenvalue decomposition to analyse repeated game dynamics, including transition matrices for strategy evolution, dominant eigenvalue and long-run behaviour, spectral analysis of Markov strategies, and convergence rates."
author: "Raban Heller"
date: 2026-05-08
date-modified: 2026-05-08
categories:
  - linear-algebra-matrix
  - eigenvalues
  - repeated-games
  - Markov-chains
keywords: ["eigenvalue decomposition", "repeated games", "Markov chain", "convergence rate", "spectral analysis"]
labels: ["linear-algebra", "dynamics"]
tier: 1
bibliography: ../../references.bib
vgwort: "TODO_VGWORT_linear-algebra-matrix_eigenvalue-methods-repeated-games"
image: thumbnail.png
image-alt: "Line plot showing strategy frequency evolution over rounds of a repeated game converging to steady state with eigenvalue decay rates using the Okabe-Ito palette."
citation:
  type: webpage
  url: https://r-heller.github.io/equilibria/tutorials/linear-algebra-matrix/eigenvalue-methods-repeated-games/
license: "CC BY-SA 4.0"
draft: false
has_static_fig: true
has_interactive_fig: true
has_shiny_app: false
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(plotly)
okabe_ito <- c("#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#F0E442",
               "#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#CC79A7", "#999999")
theme_publication <- function(base_size = 12) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(size = base_size * 1.2, face = "bold"),
          plot.subtitle = element_text(size = base_size * 0.9, color = "grey40"),
          axis.line = element_line(color = "grey30", linewidth = 0.3),
          panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom",
          plot.margin = margin(10, 10, 10, 10))
}
```

## Introduction & motivation

Repeated games are among the most important models in game theory because they capture the dynamics of ongoing strategic relationships. When the same players interact repeatedly, behaviour evolves over time as players learn from past outcomes, build reputations, and respond to opponents' histories. This evolution can be modelled as a Markov chain over the state space of strategy profiles, where the transition probabilities are determined by the players' learning rules or behavioural strategies. Eigenvalue decomposition of the resulting transition matrix provides a powerful analytical toolkit for understanding the long-run behaviour of these dynamics without simulating the process step by step.

The connection between eigenvalues and repeated game dynamics is both elegant and practical. The dominant eigenvalue of a stochastic transition matrix is always 1, and its corresponding eigenvector gives the stationary distribution -- the long-run frequency of each strategy profile. The second-largest eigenvalue determines the rate of convergence to this stationary distribution: the closer it is to 1, the slower the convergence, and the longer the transient dynamics persist before the system settles into its steady state. Higher-order eigenvalues capture oscillatory components in the transient behaviour, revealing cyclical patterns in strategy frequencies that may not be apparent from direct simulation.

This spectral perspective has been applied to some of the most fundamental questions in game theory. In the repeated Prisoner's Dilemma, the transition matrix encodes how players switch between cooperation and defection based on the history of play. The eigenvalue structure reveals whether cooperation is sustainable in the long run, how quickly a population converges to its equilibrium mix of cooperators and defectors, and what kinds of perturbations cause the largest transient deviations from steady state. In evolutionary game theory, the replicator dynamic on finite populations generates a transition matrix whose spectral properties determine fixation probabilities and absorption times. In mechanism design, the convergence rate of iterative auction formats depends on the eigenvalues of the bid update operator.

The practical utility of eigenvalue methods lies in their ability to compress complex dynamical information into a few key numbers. Rather than tracking strategy frequencies over hundreds or thousands of rounds, we can characterise the entire trajectory by the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the transition matrix. The spectral gap (the difference between the two largest eigenvalue moduli) is a single number that quantifies how quickly the system forgets its initial conditions and converges to steady-state behaviour. Large spectral gaps indicate rapid mixing, meaning the system quickly reaches its long-run distribution regardless of starting conditions. Small spectral gaps indicate slow mixing, where initial conditions leave a lasting imprint on the dynamics and transient behaviour dominates for many rounds.

This tutorial implements the complete eigenvalue analysis of a repeated game. We construct a four-state Markov chain representing the dynamics of a repeated Prisoner's Dilemma with reactive strategies (where each player's action depends on the opponent's previous action). We compute the transition matrix, perform eigenvalue decomposition, extract the stationary distribution and convergence rate, and visualise the strategy evolution over time alongside the eigenvalue-predicted convergence envelope. The analysis demonstrates how linear algebra transforms a dynamic problem into a static one, revealing the structure of long-run behaviour through the spectrum of a single matrix.

The methodology generalises broadly. Any learning dynamic that can be represented as a linear map on the space of state distributions -- including fictitious play with fading memory, reinforcement learning with fixed learning rates, and imitation dynamics -- admits eigenvalue analysis. Even nonlinear dynamics can be linearised around their fixed points, and the eigenvalues of the Jacobian matrix determine local stability. The tools of linear algebra are thus fundamental to the game theorist's analytical toolkit.


## Mathematical formulation

Consider a repeated game with $k$ joint action profiles. Under reactive strategies, the game dynamics form a Markov chain with transition matrix $P \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times k}$ where $P_{ij} = \Pr(\text{state } j \text{ at } t+1 \mid \text{state } i \text{ at } t)$. The state distribution evolves as:

$$
\boldsymbol{\pi}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\pi}_t \cdot P
$$

The eigenvalue decomposition $P = V \Lambda V^{-1}$ yields:

$$
\boldsymbol{\pi}_t = \boldsymbol{\pi}_0 \cdot V \Lambda^t V^{-1}
$$

where $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_k)$ with $|\lambda_1| \geq |\lambda_2| \geq \cdots \geq |\lambda_k|$. For an ergodic chain, $\lambda_1 = 1$ and $|\lambda_2| < 1$. The stationary distribution is the left eigenvector corresponding to $\lambda_1 = 1$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\pi}^* P = \boldsymbol{\pi}^*, \quad \sum_i \pi_i^* = 1
$$

The convergence rate is governed by $|\lambda_2|$, and the mixing time scales as:

$$
t_{\text{mix}} \sim \frac{1}{1 - |\lambda_2|}
$$


## R implementation

We construct the transition matrix for a repeated Prisoner's Dilemma with reactive strategies, perform eigenvalue decomposition, and simulate the strategy evolution to validate the spectral predictions.

```{r}
#| label: eigenvalue-analysis

set.seed(42)

# Reactive strategy parameters
# Player 1: prob of C given opponent's last move
p1_c_after_c <- 0.9   # Tit-for-Tat like: cooperate if opponent cooperated
p1_c_after_d <- 0.3   # some forgiveness
# Player 2: prob of C given opponent's last move
p2_c_after_c <- 0.8
p2_c_after_d <- 0.2

# States: CC, CD, DC, DD (row player action first)
# Transition matrix P[i,j] = Pr(state j | state i)
P <- matrix(0, 4, 4)
state_names <- c("CC", "CD", "DC", "DD")
rownames(P) <- colnames(P) <- state_names

# From CC: both saw C last round
P["CC", "CC"] <- p1_c_after_c * p2_c_after_c
P["CC", "CD"] <- p1_c_after_c * (1 - p2_c_after_c)
P["CC", "DC"] <- (1 - p1_c_after_c) * p2_c_after_c
P["CC", "DD"] <- (1 - p1_c_after_c) * (1 - p2_c_after_c)

# From CD: P1 saw D (from P2), P2 saw C (from P1)
P["CD", "CC"] <- p1_c_after_d * p2_c_after_c
P["CD", "CD"] <- p1_c_after_d * (1 - p2_c_after_c)
P["CD", "DC"] <- (1 - p1_c_after_d) * p2_c_after_c
P["CD", "DD"] <- (1 - p1_c_after_d) * (1 - p2_c_after_c)

# From DC: P1 saw C (from P2), P2 saw D (from P1)
P["DC", "CC"] <- p1_c_after_c * p2_c_after_d
P["DC", "CD"] <- p1_c_after_c * (1 - p2_c_after_d)
P["DC", "DC"] <- (1 - p1_c_after_c) * p2_c_after_d
P["DC", "DD"] <- (1 - p1_c_after_c) * (1 - p2_c_after_d)

# From DD: both saw D last round
P["DD", "CC"] <- p1_c_after_d * p2_c_after_d
P["DD", "CD"] <- p1_c_after_d * (1 - p2_c_after_d)
P["DD", "DC"] <- (1 - p1_c_after_d) * p2_c_after_d
P["DD", "DD"] <- (1 - p1_c_after_d) * (1 - p2_c_after_d)

# Eigenvalue decomposition
eig <- eigen(t(P))  # left eigenvectors of P = right eigenvectors of P^T
eigenvalues <- eig$values
eigenvectors <- eig$vectors

# Stationary distribution (eigenvector for lambda=1)
stat_idx <- which.min(abs(eigenvalues - 1))
pi_star <- Re(eigenvectors[, stat_idx])
pi_star <- pi_star / sum(pi_star)

# Convergence rate
lambda2 <- sort(abs(eigenvalues), decreasing = TRUE)[2]
mixing_time <- ceiling(1 / (1 - lambda2))

# Simulate evolution from DD (worst start)
n_rounds <- 60
pi_t <- matrix(0, n_rounds + 1, 4)
colnames(pi_t) <- state_names
pi_t[1, ] <- c(0, 0, 0, 1)  # start from DD

for (t in 1:n_rounds) {
  pi_t[t + 1, ] <- pi_t[t, ] %*% P
}

# Eigenvalue-predicted convergence envelope
dist_from_stationary <- apply(pi_t, 1, function(row)
  sqrt(sum((row - pi_star)^2)))
theoretical_envelope <- dist_from_stationary[1] * lambda2^(0:n_rounds)

cat("Transition Matrix Eigenvalues:\n")
for (i in 1:4) {
  cat(sprintf("  lambda_%d = %s (|lambda| = %.4f)\n", i,
              ifelse(Im(eigenvalues[i]) == 0,
                     sprintf("%.4f", Re(eigenvalues[i])),
                     sprintf("%.4f + %.4fi", Re(eigenvalues[i]), Im(eigenvalues[i]))),
              abs(eigenvalues[i])))
}
cat(sprintf("\nStationary distribution:\n"))
for (i in 1:4) {
  cat(sprintf("  pi(%s) = %.4f\n", state_names[i], pi_star[i]))
}
cat(sprintf("\nSpectral gap: %.4f\n", 1 - lambda2))
cat(sprintf("Second eigenvalue |lambda_2|: %.4f\n", lambda2))
cat(sprintf("Estimated mixing time: %d rounds\n", mixing_time))
coop_p1 <- pi_star[1] + pi_star[2]
coop_p2 <- pi_star[1] + pi_star[3]
cat(sprintf("P1 cooperation rate: %.1f%%, P2 cooperation rate: %.1f%%\n",
            100 * coop_p1, 100 * coop_p2))
```


## Static publication-ready figure

The figure shows the evolution of state probabilities over rounds alongside the eigenvalue-predicted convergence envelope, demonstrating how spectral analysis captures the dynamics.

```{r}
#| label: fig-eigenvalue-dynamics-static
#| fig-cap: "Figure 1. Strategy evolution in a repeated Prisoner's Dilemma with reactive strategies, starting from mutual defection (DD). Solid lines show the probability of each joint action profile over 60 rounds. The dashed grey line shows the theoretical convergence envelope predicted by the second eigenvalue. The system converges to a stationary distribution dominated by mutual cooperation (CC), with convergence rate determined by the spectral gap of the transition matrix. Okabe-Ito palette."
#| dev: [png, pdf]
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 5
#| dpi: 300

evolution_df <- as.data.frame(pi_t) %>%
  mutate(round = 0:n_rounds) %>%
  pivot_longer(-round, names_to = "state", values_to = "probability") %>%
  mutate(state = factor(state, levels = state_names))

envelope_df <- data.frame(
  round = 0:n_rounds,
  distance = dist_from_stationary,
  envelope = theoretical_envelope
)

stat_df <- data.frame(state = state_names, pi = pi_star)

p <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = evolution_df,
            aes(x = round, y = probability, colour = state,
                text = paste0("Round: ", round,
                              "\nState: ", state,
                              "\nProbability: ", round(probability, 4))),
            linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_hline(data = stat_df,
             aes(yintercept = pi), linetype = "dotted",
             colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = okabe_ito[1:4], name = "Joint action") +
  labs(title = "Strategy evolution in repeated Prisoner's Dilemma",
       subtitle = paste0("Reactive strategies | Spectral gap: ",
                         round(1 - lambda2, 3),
                         " | Mixing time: ~", mixing_time, " rounds"),
       x = "Round", y = "State probability") +
  theme_publication()
p
```


## Interactive figure

Hover over the evolution curves to track the exact probability of each joint action profile at every round. The interactive display reveals the transient dynamics predicted by the subdominant eigenvalues.

```{r}
#| label: fig-eigenvalue-dynamics-interactive

ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text") |>
  config(displaylogo = FALSE,
         modeBarButtonsToRemove = c("select2d", "lasso2d"))
```


## Interpretation

The eigenvalue analysis of the repeated Prisoner's Dilemma reveals a rich dynamical picture that would be difficult to obtain by simulation alone. Starting from mutual defection (DD), the system converges to a stationary distribution heavily weighted toward mutual cooperation (CC). This convergence is not coincidental but reflects the underlying strategic logic of the reactive strategies: both players are configured to cooperate with high probability after observing cooperation and with moderate probability even after defection. The Tit-for-Tat-like strategy profile creates a positive feedback loop where cooperation begets cooperation, and the eigenvalue analysis quantifies exactly how quickly this feedback drives the system to its cooperative steady state.

The spectral gap, defined as $1 - |\lambda_2|$, is the single most informative quantity extracted from the eigenvalue decomposition. A large spectral gap means rapid convergence to the stationary distribution, implying that the initial conditions are quickly forgotten and the long-run behaviour dominates. In our example, the spectral gap determines how many rounds of play are needed before the system effectively "forgets" that it started in the DD state and behaves as if it had always been in steady state. This has practical implications for experimental game theory: if the spectral gap is small, short laboratory sessions may not be long enough for subjects to reach steady-state behaviour, and observed cooperation rates will depend heavily on the initial conditions of the experiment.

The transient dynamics, captured by the subdominant eigenvalues and eigenvectors, are equally informative. When eigenvalues are real and positive, the convergence to steady state is monotonic -- each state probability moves smoothly toward its stationary value without overshooting. When eigenvalues are negative or complex, the transient dynamics exhibit oscillations. Negative real eigenvalues produce alternating behaviour where a state probability overshoots its target, then undershoots, then overshoots again, with decaying amplitude. Complex eigenvalues produce damped oscillations with a specific frequency determined by the argument of the eigenvalue. In the context of repeated games, oscillatory transients correspond to cyclical patterns of cooperation and defection that gradually dampen as the system approaches steady state.

The stationary distribution itself provides the game-theoretic prediction for long-run behaviour under the specified reactive strategies. The decomposition into eigenmodes explains why some initial conditions converge faster than others: initial distributions that are aligned with the stationary eigenvector are already at steady state, while those aligned with the second eigenvector experience the slowest convergence. This insight guides experimental design -- if we want to observe steady-state behaviour quickly, we should start the game in a state close to the stationary distribution rather than in an extreme state like DD.

The methodology extends naturally to more complex settings. For games with more than two players, the state space grows exponentially, but the eigenvalue analysis remains conceptually identical -- only the matrix grows larger. For continuous-action games, the transition operator becomes infinite-dimensional, and the eigenvalue analysis is replaced by spectral theory for operators. The key insight persists: the long-run behaviour of repeated strategic interactions is encoded in the spectral properties of the transition dynamics, and linear algebra provides the tools to extract this information efficiently.

One important caveat concerns the assumption of fixed reactive strategies. In practice, players may adapt their strategies over time based on accumulated experience, which means the transition matrix itself evolves. Eigenvalue analysis of a fixed matrix captures the dynamics conditional on fixed strategies, but a complete model of adaptive play requires additional tools such as stochastic approximation theory or reinforcement learning models. Nevertheless, the fixed-strategy analysis provides the foundation: the steady states and convergence rates under fixed strategies determine the fitness landscape that drives strategy adaptation.


## Extensions & related tutorials

Eigenvalue methods extend to multi-player repeated games via tensor decompositions, to continuous strategy spaces via spectral operator theory, and to adaptive dynamics via the study of time-varying Markov chains. The connection between spectral gaps and mixing times has deep applications in algorithmic game theory, particularly in the complexity of computing equilibria through sampling methods.

- [Key player identification in network games](../../network-games/key-player-identification/)
- [Genetic algorithms for equilibrium computation](../../optimization-numerical-methods/genetic-algorithm-equilibria/)
- [Unit testing game theory solvers in R](../../r-package-development/unit-testing-game-solvers/)
- [GANs as a game-theoretic training process](../../ml-and-gt/gan-game-theoretic-training/)
- [World trade data for strategic analysis](../../public-apis-and-datasets/world-trade-strategic-data/)


## References

::: {#refs}
:::
